title: Salting Out Purification of β-Lactoglobulin from Whey Protein Isolate
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"6jub3","text":"Method for isolating β-lactoglobulin from whey protein isolate by \"salting out\" of α-lactalbumin and immunoglobulins. Adapted from work done by P. Mailliart \u0026  B. Ribadeau-Dumans (1988). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: In a 250 mL Erlenmeyer flask, add 24 g Whey Protein Isolate n/a to 160 mL Deionized Water Merck Millipore (CAS: 7732-18-5) under agitation (15% w/v). Use a large stir bar and a high agitation speed so the protein is well-dispersed.
Once most of the protein is dissolved, use Hydrochloric Acid Fisher Scientific (CAS: 7647-01-0) to adjust the solution to pH 2.0.
Add 11.2 g Sodium Chloride VWR (7% w/v) to the solution under agitation. Some precipitate will form.
Allow the solution to sit for 20 minutes to allow the protein to fully precipitate.
Split the solution into four 50 mL centrifuge tubes. Centrifuge the solution at 3220 RCF (maximum speed) for at least 10 minutes.

Desalt the solution by dialyzing against 3L of deionized water (12-14 kDa MWCO membranes recommended), changing the water at least four times with at least 3 hours between water changes. Make sure to stir the dialysis bath. Author's Note: I recommend using a 4 liter beaker for this step. When skipping the washing step, I cut four standard-size dialysis tubes to reach from the bottom of the beaker to the 3 L mark. That should give enough length to fit the solution and secure the ends of the tubes.
Pour the dialyzed solution into 50 mL centrifuge tubes. Freeze in liquid nitrogen (preferred) or a -80°C freezer, then lyophilize the contents until visibly dry (48-72h).
OPTIONAL: Wash the precipitate with 6% NaCl (w/v) at pH 2.0 to recover any entrapped β-lactoglobulin. Centrifuge and collect the supernatant again.
creator: Andrew E Ashmar
